sections.title: Useful notes
items: "I **have to** finish this before tomorrow," he said.
Passengers **must** keep their bags with them at all times.

items.correct: You **mustn't** open that door! (= It is forbidden — do not do it.)
You **don't have to** open that door. (= You have a choice — it is not necessary.)

sections.title: Useful notes
items: His coat is here but he isn't. He **must** have stepped outside for a moment.

sections.title: Be careful
items: To speculate about past events, use a modal followed by the perfect infinitive (have + past participle).

exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Complete with must, have to, need to or mustn't
exercises.title: A: Complete with must, have to, need to or mustn't
exercises.instructions: Write the best modal form in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write the best modal form in each gap.

items.sentence: You ...... show your ticket before entering the stadium.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is strong obligation/rule, so the correct answer is "must".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: We ...... hurry or the shop will close.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is external necessity, so the correct answer is "have to".

wrongOptions.to: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: I ...... send this form before midnight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is necessity, so the correct answer is "need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Guests ...... take food into the swimming pool area.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is prohibition, so the correct answer is "mustn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: All players ...... wear the correct boots for the match.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rule, so the correct answer is "have to".

wrongOptions.to: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... open that file. It may contain a virus.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is prohibition/warning, so the correct answer is "mustn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: I ...... remember to lock the back door tonight.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is personal obligation, so the correct answer is "must".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Do we ...... arrive before eight?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is question about necessity, so the correct answer is "have to".

wrongOptions.to: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

exercises.exerciseTypeName: B: Choose don't have to, don't need to or needn't
exercises.title: B: Choose don't have to, don't need to or needn't
exercises.instructions: Complete each sentence with a form showing no obligation.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete each sentence with a form showing no obligation.

items.sentence: You ...... wash the dishes. I've already done them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not necessary, so the correct answer is "don't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: It's a bank holiday tomorrow, so Dad ...... go to work.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no obligation (bank holiday), so the correct answer is "doesn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "doesn't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... come with us if you're too tired.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is optional, so the correct answer is "needn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We ...... bring chairs; the school will provide them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not necessary, so the correct answer is "don't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "don't need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Lily ...... study French this year because she chose German.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no obligation, so the correct answer is "doesn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "doesn't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... answer now. Think about it first.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no rush, so the correct answer is "needn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "needn't" is the only word or phrase that fits both grammar and meaning in: "You ...... answer now. Think about it first.".

items.sentence: I ...... get a taxi because my brother can drive me.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is alternative available, so the correct answer is "don't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "don't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... write your name twice on the form.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is unnecessary, so the correct answer is "don't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "don't need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: C: Past obligation
exercises.instructions: Complete using had to, didn't have to or didn't need to.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete using had to, didn't have to or didn't need to.

items.sentence: We ...... wait outside because the doors were locked.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past obligation, so the correct answer is "had to".

wrongOptions.to: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: I ...... take my umbrella because it didn't rain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no past obligation, so the correct answer is "didn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Alex ...... finish his homework before dinner yesterday.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past obligation, so the correct answer is "had to".

wrongOptions.to: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: They ...... wear sports clothes because the match was cancelled.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not necessary, so the correct answer is "didn't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Passive voice focuses on the action or object, not who performs it.

items.sentence: I ...... ask for directions; I had a map.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is had a map, so the correct answer is "didn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: Clara ...... stay in hospital for two nights.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past obligation, so the correct answer is "had to".

wrongOptions.to: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: We ...... buy tickets. My uncle had already bought them.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is tickets already bought, so the correct answer is "didn't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple only shows one past event — use past perfect when one past action happened before another.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past perfect describes an action that happened before another past action.

items.sentence: Tom ...... get up early on Sunday, so he slept until ten.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no obligation on Sunday, so the correct answer is "didn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

exercises.title: D: Circle the correct phrase
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct option in each sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct option in each sentence.

items.sentenceAfter: come if you have other plans.
items.originalSentence: You **mustn't / don't have to** come if you have other plans.
items.completedSentence: You don't have to come if you have other plans.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: There is no obligation — the action is optional, not forbidden.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Don't have to = no obligation. Mustn't = prohibition (not allowed).

wrongOptions.don't have to: Don't have to means optional — it does not mean forbidden.
wrongOptions.mustn't: Mustn't means something is forbidden, not merely optional.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Mustn't is one of the strongest ways to say something is not allowed.

optionContrast.mustn't: "mustn't" doesn't fit here → "don't have to" does, because Mustn't means something is forbidden, not merely optional.

items.sentenceAfter: touch the paintings in the gallery.
items.originalSentence: You **mustn't / don't have to** touch the paintings in the gallery.
items.completedSentence: You mustn't touch the paintings in the gallery.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The situation requires a strong prohibition or ban.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Don't have to = no obligation. Mustn't = prohibition (not allowed).

wrongOptions.don't have to: Don't have to means optional — it does not mean forbidden.
wrongOptions.mustn't: Mustn't means something is forbidden, not merely optional.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Mustn't is one of the strongest ways to say something is not allowed.

optionContrast.don't have to: "don't have to" doesn't fit here → "mustn't" does, because Don't have to means optional — it does not mean forbidden.

items.sentenceAfter: wait long because the bus arrived immediately.
items.originalSentence: We **had to / didn't have to** wait long because the bus arrived immediately.
items.completedSentence: We didn't have to wait long because the bus arrived immediately.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: No waiting was necessary — the action was not required.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Didn't have to = it was not necessary. Had to = it was necessary / obligatory.

wrongOptions.didn't have to: Didn't have to means unnecessary — not the same as had to.
wrongOptions.had to: Had to means obligation — not the same as no need to wait.

optionContrast.had to: "had to" doesn't fit here → "didn't have to" does, because Had to means obligation — not the same as no need to wait.

items.sentenceAfter: buy a birthday present for my sister.
items.originalSentence: I **need to / mustn't** buy a birthday present for my sister.
items.completedSentence: I need to buy a birthday present for my sister.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "need to" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "need to" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "mustn't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.mustn't: "mustn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("buy a birthday present for my sister.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.mustn't: "mustn't" doesn't fit here → "need to" does, because "mustn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("buy a birthday present for my sister.").

items.sentenceAfter: wear a badge in this building.
items.originalSentence: All visitors **must / needn't** wear a badge in this building.
items.completedSentence: All visitors must wear a badge in this building.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "must" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "must" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "needn't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.needn't: "needn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("wear a badge in this building.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.needn't: "needn't" doesn't fit here → "must" does, because "needn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("wear a badge in this building.").

items.sentenceAfter: call a mechanic when her car stopped.
items.originalSentence: Nina **had to / didn't have to** call a mechanic when her car stopped.
items.completedSentence: Nina had to call a mechanic when her car stopped.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Waiting was necessary in this situation.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Didn't have to = it was not necessary. Had to = it was necessary / obligatory.

wrongOptions.didn't have to: Didn't have to means unnecessary — not the same as had to.
wrongOptions.had to: Had to means obligation — not the same as no need to wait.

optionContrast.didn't have to: "didn't have to" doesn't fit here → "had to" does, because Didn't have to means unnecessary — not the same as had to.

items.originalSentence: You **needn't / mustn't** explain it again — I understand.
items.completedSentence: You needn't explain it again — I understand.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Only "needn't" fits the meaning created by the words before and after the gap.
explanationContent.vocabularyFocus: "needn't" collocates with the rest of the sentence. "mustn't" would change the meaning or is the wrong register for this context.

wrongOptions.mustn't: "mustn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("explain it again — I understand.").

explanationContent.usefulTip: When two words look similar, read the whole sentence — the clue is usually before or after the gap.

optionContrast.mustn't: "mustn't" doesn't fit here → "needn't" does, because "mustn't" does not match the clue in the rest of the sentence ("explain it again — I understand.").

items.originalSentence: We **don't have to / mustn't** lose these documents.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The situation requires a strong prohibition or ban.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Don't have to = no obligation. Mustn't = prohibition (not allowed).

wrongOptions.don't have to: Don't have to means optional — it does not mean forbidden.
wrongOptions.mustn't: Mustn't means something is forbidden, not merely optional.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Mustn't is one of the strongest ways to say something is not allowed.

optionContrast.don't have to: "don't have to" doesn't fit here → "mustn't" does, because Don't have to means optional — it does not mean forbidden.

exercises.title: E: Probability and possibility
exercises.instructions: Complete with must, can't, couldn't, should, ought to, could, may or might.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete with must, can't, couldn't, should, ought to, could, may or might.

items.sentence: That ...... be Omar. He always wears that red jacket.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is logical certainty, so the correct answer is "must".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: This ...... be Anna's bag — she never carries a backpack.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is impossible, so the correct answer is "can't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence: The meeting ...... finish late if there are many questions.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possible, so the correct answer is "might".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: Your parcel ...... arrive tomorrow morning.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is expectation, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: I'm not sure where Dad is. He ...... be in the garage.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possibility, so the correct answer is "could".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence: The shop is closed and the lights are off. It ...... be open.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is impossible (closed), so the correct answer is "couldn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modal verbs express different degrees of possibility, ability, and permission.

items.sentence: The team has trained hard, so they ...... win the match.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is expected result, so the correct answer is "ought to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "ought to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: Take a jumper — it ...... get cold later.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possibility, so the correct answer is "may".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

exercises.title: F: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one modal verb mistake. Write the correct modal.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one modal verb mistake. Write the correct modal.

items.sentence: You look ill. You must see a doctor.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "must" — advice, not obligation, so the correction is "should".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.must: "must" is the highlighted error — use "should" here instead.
wrongOptions.obligation: "obligation" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "should".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "must" — the rule here needs "should" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: This should be Daniel's notebook. His name is on the cover.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "should" — strong certainty, so the correction is "must".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.should: "should" is the highlighted error — use "must" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "should" — the rule here needs "must" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: We must bring lunch; food is provided at the camp.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "must" — food provided, so the correction is "don't have to".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.must: "must" is the highlighted error — use "don't have to" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "must", not elsewhere — replace it with "don't have to" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: You needn't touch those chemicals. They are dangerous.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "needn't" — dangerous, so the correction is "mustn't".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.needn't: "needn't" is the highlighted error — use "mustn't" here instead.
wrongOptions.needn: "needn" does not fit this slot — the correct word is "mustn't".

explanationContent.commonMistake: Do not leave "needn't" — the rule here needs "mustn't" in the highlighted slot.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: She left an hour ago, so she can't arrive soon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "can't" — left an hour ago, so the correction is "should".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.can't: "can't" is the highlighted error — use "should" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "can't", not elsewhere — replace it with "should" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

items.sentence: He mustn't work late yesterday because the deadline was today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The error is in "mustn't" — past obligation, so the correction is "had to".
explanationContent.grammarFocus: The highlighted word breaks the collocation or meaning — choose the word that fits naturally in this context.

wrongOptions.mustn't: "mustn't" is the highlighted error — use "had to" here instead.

explanationContent.commonMistake: The error is in "mustn't", not elsewhere — replace it with "had to" and read the full sentence to check it sounds natural.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the sentence with your correction inserted — if it still sounds wrong, you may have changed the wrong word.

exercises.title: G: Error correction
exercises.instructions: Each sentence has one modal error. Write the correct form.
exercises.studentInstruction: Each sentence has one modal error. Write the correct form.

items.displayPrompt: You don't have to cross the road when the light is red.

cues: You don't have to cross the road when the light is red.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Red light = prohibition, so the sentence is "mustn't".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: You don't have to cross the road when the light is red. → mustn't.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Time clauses set the order of events — match verb tenses to show which action happened first.

wrongOptions.Red: Check how "Red" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.light: Check how "light" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: I must to finish this exercise today.

cues: I must to finish this exercise today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Must + base verb, so the sentence is "must finish".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: I must to finish this exercise today. → must finish.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Build a grammatically complete sentence that matches the prompt: must finish.

wrongOptions.Must: Check how "Must" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: We had to not bring books to the trip.

cues: We had to not bring books to the trip.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: No obligation, so the sentence is "didn't have to bring".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: We had to not bring books to the trip. → didn't have to bring.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Negative sentences need the correct auxiliary + not (hasn't, hadn't, don't) before the main verb.

wrongOptions.No: Check how "No" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.obligation: Check how "obligation" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: That must be Sophie; she's on holiday in Portugal.

cues: That must be Sophie; she's on holiday in Portugal.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: On holiday in Portugal, so the sentence is "can't".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: That must be Sophie; she's on holiday in Portugal. → can't.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Negative sentences need the correct auxiliary + not (hasn't, hadn't, don't) before the main verb.

wrongOptions.On: Check how "On" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.
wrongOptions.holiday: Check how "holiday" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: You needn't to pay for the museum today.

cues: You needn't to pay for the museum today.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Needn't + base verb, so the sentence is "needn't pay".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: You needn't to pay for the museum today. → needn't pay.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Negative sentences need the correct auxiliary + not (hasn't, hadn't, don't) before the main verb.

wrongOptions.Needn't: Check how "Needn't" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Match the tense shift shown in the explanation — one verb often moves back one step in time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

items.displayPrompt: This may is the correct password, but I'm not certain.

cues: This may is the correct password, but I'm not certain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: Possibility, so the sentence is "may be".
explanationContent.sentenceBreakdown: Prompt: This may is the correct password, but I'm not certain. → may be.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Negative sentences need the correct auxiliary + not (hasn't, hadn't, don't) before the main verb.

wrongOptions.Possibility: Check how "Possibility" changes in the target sentence — tense or word form may shift.

explanationContent.commonMistake: In reported speech, change time and place words (tomorrow → the next day, here → there).
explanationContent.usefulTip: Write one complete sentence with correct punctuation — capital letter at the start and a full stop at the end.

exercises.title: H: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given.

items.sentence:
It's necessary for passengers to fasten their seatbelts. **MUST**
Passengers ...... their seatbelts.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is necessary, so the correct answer is "must fasten".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "must fasten", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
It wasn't necessary for me to phone the office. **HAVE**
I ...... to phone the office.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not necessary, so the correct answer is "didn't have".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "didn't have", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
I'm almost sure that's Maya's umbrella. **MUST**
That ...... Maya's umbrella.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is certainty, so the correct answer is "must be".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "must be", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
Maybe they'll visit us next weekend. **MIGHT**
They ...... us next weekend.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possibility, so the correct answer is "might visit".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "might visit", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modal verbs express different degrees of possibility, ability, and permission.

items.sentence:
It's prohibited to enter this area. **MUSTN'T**
You ...... this area.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is prohibited, so the correct answer is "mustn't enter".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "mustn't enter", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
It's probable that the train will arrive soon. **SHOULD**
The train ...... soon.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is probable, so the correct answer is "should arrive".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "should arrive", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

exercises.title: I: Mixed revision
exercises.instructions: Write one suitable modal form in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one suitable modal form in each gap.

items.sentence: You ...... bring a towel; the hotel provides one.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is hotel provides towels, so the correct answer is "don't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "don't have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: The film starts in ten minutes, so we ...... go now.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is urgent, so the correct answer is "have to".

wrongOptions.to: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "have to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: His phone is on the table, so he ...... be far away.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is phone on table, so the correct answer is "can't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Try saying the phrase aloud — the correct preposition is usually part of a fixed pattern.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: I ...... finish the poster today. The deadline is next week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is deadline next week, so the correct answer is "don't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "don't need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence: She practised a lot, so she ...... do well in the concert.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lots of practice, so the correct answer is "should".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: You ...... feed the animals in the park. It's forbidden.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is forbidden, so the correct answer is "mustn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: The car is in the drive, so Dad ...... be home.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is car in drive, so the correct answer is "must".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Compare your answer with the grammar or vocabulary clue in the sentence — tense, form, or collocation may be wrong.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence: We ...... walk yesterday because Mum picked us up.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is mum picked us up, so the correct answer is "didn't have to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence: I'm not sure, but this key ...... open the side door.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is uncertain, so the correct answer is "might".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check whether the noun is specific, countable, or mentioned for the first time.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence: The lift was broken, so we ...... use the stairs.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is lift broken, so the correct answer is "had to".

wrongOptions.to: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "had to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Conditional sentences pair specific verb forms in the if-clause and main clause.

exercises.title: J: Key word transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using a maximum of four words, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence.

items.sentence:
There is no need for you to bring your dictionary.
...... bring your dictionary.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is no need, so the correct answer is "You don't need to".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "You don't need to", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
The rules say you cannot eat in this room.
You ...... in this room.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is rules forbid it, so the correct answer is "mustn't eat".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "mustn't eat", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
I'm certain he is in the garden now.
He ...... in the garden.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is certain, so the correct answer is "must be".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "must be", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Modals express advice, obligation, permission, or prohibition — choose the nuance that matches the sentence.

items.sentence:
It was necessary for every player to arrive early.
Every player ...... early.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past obligation, so the correct answer is "had to arrive".

wrongOptions.to arrive: Without had, the earlier-past relationship is lost — use past perfect for the first of two past actions.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

items.sentence:
Perhaps she missed the bus.
She ...... the bus.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is possibility in past, so the correct answer is "might have missed".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "might have missed", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
You are not required to bring your own lunch.
...... bring your own lunch.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is not required, so the correct answer is "You needn't".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "You needn't", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.
